concepts.title: Introduction to collisions
concepts.conceptBody:
Collision

Contrary to the meaning of the term 'collision' in our everyday life, in physics it does not necessarily mean one particle 'striking' against other. Indeed two particles may not even touch each other and may still be said to collide. All that is implied is that as the particles approach each other.

(i) an impulse (a large force for a relatively short time) acts on each colliding particles

(ii) the total momentum of the particles remain conserved.

Now, let us have a look at various stages of collision between two bodies

During collision, the two bodies interact with each other with large impulsive force, leading to finite change in their momentum. 
 

Note : In collisions and explosions, neglect the effect of the constant forces for very short time intervals, unless the time of impact is mentioned.

I. For example, let us analyze an illustration where the body explodes. When it is at maximum height.

Due to very short time, the effect of constant force mg can be neglected.

Hence, momentum of the system can be conserved in all directions.

II. Here we analyze a collision between a big body with a small body.

Here, normal reactions at point $\text{A}$ and base impulsive in nature.

Tip : Any force which can be expressed in terms of impulsive forces, is also impulsive.

Here, momentum cannot be conserved in vertical directions.

III. Now, analyze a collision between similar bodies both moving horizontally.

Momentum can be conserved in any direction,

$\therefore {{\text{N}}_{1}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{N}}_{2}},\mu {{\text{N}}_{1}},\mu {{\text{N}}_{2}}$ are all constant forces and hence can be neglected.

IV. Analyzing 2 similar bodies when one is kept on ground

Momentum of the system cannot be conserved in vertical direction as here also $\text{N}$ is impulsive.

concepts.title: Head on and oblique collision
concepts.conceptBody:
Head-On and Oblique Collision 

Head-On Collisions : If the velocities of point of contact of two bodies before collision coincide with line of impact.

Oblique Collision : If the velocity of point of contact of any of the colliding bodies, does not lie along the common normal, then it is oblique collision.

Note : If there is no friction at the surfaces of contact, then the component of velocities perpendicular to common normal remains unchanged.

We can see here that an impulsive force $\text{N}$ acts on both the bodies in opposite directions.

 
 

We assume that the body $A$ acquires a horizontal velocity ${{\text{v}}_{1}}$ and $\text{B}$ acquires a horizontal velocity ${{\text{v}}_{2}}$. Please not the vertical component of the velocities remain unchanged as there was no force in vertical direction.

concepts.title: Newton's law of restitution
concepts.conceptBody:
Newton's Law of Restitution 

When two objects are in direct (head on) impact, the speed with which they separate after impact is usually less than or equal to their speed of approach before impact.

Experimental evidence suggests that the ratio of these relative speeds is constant for two given set of objects. This property formulated by Newton, is known as the law of restitution and can be written in the form

$\frac{\text{ separation }\text{ speed }}{\text{ approach }\text{ speed }}=\text{e}$

The ratio e is called the coefficient of restitution and is constant for two particular objects. 
In general 
$0\le \text{e}\le 1$

$\text{e}=0$, for completely inelastic collision, as both the objects stick together. So, their separation speed is zero or $\text{e}=0$ from (1).

$\text{e}=1$, for an elastic collision, as we can show that

$\text{v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}={{\text{u}}_{1}}\text{cos}{{\theta }_{1}}+{{\text{u}}_{2}}\text{cos}{{\theta }_{2}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ separation speed = approach speed

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ e}=1$

Let us now find the velocities of two particles after collision if they collide directly and the coefficient of restitution between them is given as e.

Applying conservation of linear momentum

${{\text{m}}_{1}}{{\text{v}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}{{\text{v}}_{2}}={{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}\text{v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}$

Further separation speed $=\text{e}($approach speed)

$\text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}-\text{v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\text{e}\left( {{\text{v}}_{2}}-{{\text{v}}_{1}} \right)$

Solving Eqs. (2) and (3), we get

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\left( \frac{{{\text{m}}_{1}}-\text{e}{{\text{m}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{1}}+\left( \frac{{{\text{m}}_{2}}-\text{e}{{\text{m}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{2}} \\ {} & \text{v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\left( \frac{{{\text{m}}_{2}}-\text{e}{{\text{m}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{2}}+\left( \frac{{{\text{m}}_{1}}-\text{e}{{\text{m}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{1}} \\ \end{array}$

Special Cases 

  1. If collision is elastic, i.e., $\text{e}=1$, then

$\text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\left( \frac{{{\text{m}}_{1}}-{{\text{m}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{1}}+\left( \frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\left( \frac{{{\text{m}}_{2}}-{{\text{m}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{2}}+\left( \frac{2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}} \right){{\text{v}}_{1}}$

  1. If collision is perfectly inelastic, i.e., $\text{e}=0$, then

$\text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\text{v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\frac{{{\text{m}}_{1}}{{\text{v}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}{{\text{v}}_{2}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}}+{{\text{m}}_{2}}}=\text{{v}' }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (say) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

  1. If ${{\text{m}}_{1}}={{\text{m}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{v}}_{1}}=0$, then

$\text{v}_{1}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\left( \frac{1+\text{e}}{2} \right){{\text{v}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}_{2}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}=\left( \frac{1-\text{e}}{2} \right){{\text{v}}_{2}}$

Remember : 

The equation from coefficient of restitution is always written along the line of impact.

$\text{e}=1$ (elastic collision)

$\theta <\text{e}<1$ (inelastic collision)

$\text{e}=0$ (perfectly inelastic collision)

How to apply the coefficient of restitution equation ? 

(1) Identify the line of impact

(2) Determine the component of velocities of points of contact of colliding bodies along the line of impact, before and after the collision.

(3) Use the equation.

For example, take the case of a particle hitting a stationary rod at distance $d$ from its centre. 
 

$\text{e}=\frac{\left( {{\text{v}}_{0}}+\text{wd} \right)-\text{v}}{\left( \text{u}-0 \right)}$

Note : 

  1. Displacement of colliding particles are neglected just before and after collision.
  2. If mass of one body is very-very greater than that of the other, then after collision velocity of heavy body does not change appreciably. (Whether the collision is elastic or inelastic).
